pattern: Only one pair of fill values ever gets chosen for the whole array, so every gap of missing entries becomes a tiny interval constraint on those two shared numbers -- binary searching the answer turns 'pick the best pair' into 'can any pair satisfy every gap at once'.
hints: Binary search the answer d: for a fixed d, check whether SOME pair of fill values can make every adjacent difference at most d, rather than trying to construct the pair directly.

A run of missing entries bounded by known neighbors p and q can be satisfied by a single fill value only when p and q are within 2d of each other; a run of two or more missing entries can instead switch fill values partway through, at the cost of tying the two fill values to each other within d.
Fixing one fill value to the smallest known entry in the array plus d is always safe, so you only need to search for a second value that satisfies every run this first value doesn't already cover.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, one gap of one missing entry
statement example, two separate single-gap runs
leading run of two missing entries needing a transition
trailing run of two missing entries
adjacent known values already far apart

stress.description: random small values with random -1 gaps
